authorship_evidence: Repository created as an intentionally vulnerable teaching app with simple Python 2 code style, pre-LLM era patterns, and manual HTML templates. No LLM markers present.

evidence.description: SQL injection via string concatenation in login query. User-supplied 'username' and 'password' parameters are concatenated directly into the SQL string: cur.execute("SELECT * FROM Users WHERE User ='" + uname + "' AND Password ='" + pwd + "'"). An attacker can bypass authentication or extract data with payloads like ' OR '1'='1' --.

OS command injection via os.popen(). The 'server' parameter from the POST request is concatenated directly into a shell command: os.popen('ping -c 3 ' + server). An attacker can inject arbitrary commands using shell metacharacters (e.g., '127.0.0.1; cat /etc/passwd').

Path traversal in ContentHandler. The 'file' query parameter is concatenated directly into a file path: io.open('read/' + file_name, 'rb'). An attacker can read arbitrary files by supplying directory traversal sequences (e.g., ?file=../../etc/passwd).

Path traversal in StaticHandler. The path captured from the URL regex r'/static/(.*)' is prepended with 'static/' and joined with the base directory without any sanitization (lines 40-42), then opened at line 46: open(static_file, 'r'). An attacker can request /static/../../etc/passwd to read arbitrary files outside the static directory.

Reflected XSS in SearchHandler. The 'q' query parameter is passed directly to self.render() as 'query' and 'link' variables. In search.html, these are rendered using {% raw query %} (line 70, HTML context) and {% raw link %} (line 71, JavaScript context), which bypasses Tornado's default auto-escaping. Additionally, X-XSS-Protection is explicitly set to '0' (line 88), disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter.

Debug mode is enabled in production settings: 'debug': True. In Tornado, debug mode enables auto-reload, serves detailed stack traces to clients on errors (information disclosure), and disables template caching. This exposes internal application details to attackers.

Multiple hardcoded credentials in create_db() function. User accounts with plaintext passwords are hardcoded in source: admin/admin (line 140), test-user/test@123 (line 141), sagar/lall0l (line 142), alias/ohrealli (line 143), jacky/st40ngp@55 (line 144), sam/tomTOM123 (line 145), maxi/D@ni3lDizaark (line 146), lelol/Leeee@Looo@!$ (line 147). These credentials are exposed in source control.

Passwords are stored in plaintext in the SQLite database. The CREATE TABLE and INSERT statements store raw password strings without any hashing: cur.execute("INSERT INTO Users VALUES(1,'admin','admin')"). An attacker who gains database access (e.g., via SQL injection) obtains all passwords in cleartext.

Missing CSRF protection. The Application settings do not include 'xsrf_cookies': True, and no CSRF tokens are used in any forms (login, upload, server ping). This allows cross-site request forgery attacks against all POST endpoints.

Unrestricted file upload in UploadHandler. The handler accepts any file type without validation of content type, extension, or file content. The original file extension is preserved (line 62: os.path.splitext(original_fname)[1]) and the file is written to /tmp/. While the filename is randomized, the lack of extension/type validation allows uploading of executable scripts, web shells, or other malicious files.

Sensitive data logged to stdout. The UsersHandler.post() method logs the full request body containing username and password credentials: print "POST ", self.request.uri, "\nBODY ", self.request.body. This exposes user credentials in application logs.

X-XSS-Protection header explicitly set to '0', disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter: self.set_header('X-XSS-Protection', '0'). This weakens client-side defenses against the reflected XSS vulnerability in the same handler.

Missing authentication on ServerHandler POST method. The handler allows any unauthenticated user to access the ping functionality which leads to OS command execution (os.popen at line 125). There is no authentication check, session validation, or access control of any kind before processing the request. This is a distinct access-control weakness from the command injection itself (GT-002) — the ground truth already separately tracks CSRF (GT-009) and command injection (GT-002) for this handler, establishing that access-control and injection findings are independent concerns. An unauthenticated attacker can directly reach the command injection sink without needing credentials.

Missing brute-force protection on the login endpoint. The UsersHandler.post() method accepts unlimited login attempts with no rate limiting, account lockout, or throttling mechanism. The handler is a plain Tornado RequestHandler with no middleware or decorator implementing any form of request throttling. Combined with the plaintext password storage (GT-008) and hardcoded credentials (GT-007), an attacker can perform unrestricted credential brute-force attacks. This is distinct from all existing ground truth entries — none cover CWE-307.

NOT VULNERABLE. The {{ msg }} expression in login.html uses Tornado's default template syntax which auto-escapes HTML entities. Unlike {% raw %}, the {{ }} syntax applies html_escape() to the output, preventing XSS. Even though 'msg' contains user-influenced data (login success/failure message with username from DB), it is safely escaped.

NOT VULNERABLE. The {{ cmd }} expression in server.html renders the user-supplied 'server' input inside an input field's value attribute. Although the data originates from user input (the server field in ServerHandler.post), Tornado's {{ }} template syntax auto-escapes HTML, preventing attribute injection or XSS.

NOT VULNERABLE. The {{ msg }} expression in server.html renders the output of the ping command inside a <pre> tag. Although the data includes command output that could theoretically contain HTML, Tornado's {{ }} template syntax auto-escapes all HTML entities, preventing stored or reflected XSS.
